title: Welcome to Our Van - 2022 Winnebago Travato 59K
tagline: Everything you need to enjoy the van. Tap any card for step-by-step instructions with photos — or open Quick Reference for the one-screen cheat sheet.

sections.title: Welcome
sections.quick: Welcome to our van — here's how everything works! Skim these cards before you head out. Tap any card for full steps and photos, or open the Quick Reference card for a one-screen cheat sheet. Call us anytime — the number is in the Emergency card.
steps: Do a walk-around outside: check tires look inflated, nothing left under the van, all exterior doors/compartments latched.
Inside, find the OnePlace monitor panel — this shows battery charge, water tank, and waste tank levels.
Locate the fire extinguisher, first-aid kit, and the two exits (side door and cab doors).
Confirm the fridge is on and cold before loading food.
Read the "Dos & Don'ts" card once — it prevents 90% of problems.
tips: When in doubt, take a photo of a switch/valve position BEFORE you change it, so you can put it back.
The full Winnebago manual and helpful walk-through videos are in the Resources card.

sections.title: Controls Location
sections.quick: ALL the main power controls are in ONE place — ABOVE THE SLIDING DOOR. That panel area has the INVERTER switch, the OnePlace monitor panel (battery charge + water/waste tank levels), and the Power Control System panel. Whenever a card says "turn on the inverter" or "check the panel," this is where you go.
steps: Look at the wall/ceiling area directly ABOVE THE SLIDING (side entry) DOOR.
Find the INVERTER switch here — this powers the TV, sound bar, and the outlet in the cabinet above the TV.
Find the OnePlace monitor panel here — shows house battery charge and fresh/gray/black tank levels.
The Power Control System panel (LCD with SELECT / SCROLL buttons) is here too.
Start here anytime you need power controls or a status check.
tips: This is the "mission control" of the van — if in doubt, look above the sliding door first.
Each panel has its own how-to card in the Panels section: OnePlace, Power Control System, and the Truma heater (the Water Center is behind the passenger rear door).

photos.caption: Control area above the sliding door (inverter switch, OnePlace + Power Control System panels)

sections.title: Before You Go
sections.quick: A quick prep checklist before you head out so you leave with full water and propane, empty waste tanks, and a cold fridge.
steps: Fill the fresh water tank (Water Center → "Power Fill Tank," then back to "Dry Camping").
Check tank status on the OnePlace panel: fresh water FULL, and the gray and black tanks EMPTY (dump them before travel days).
Check/fill the propane tank so you have enough for cooking and heat (top it off at a propane fill station).
Turn the fridge on and set it to ~3–4 the night before so it's cold; pre-chill food before loading (it cools better when it's not cooling warm items).
Load food, drinks, and supplies (see Supplies & Gear).
Charge the house battery — drive or plug into shore power so you start full.
Do the pre-drive walk-around (see Driving): roof vents and antenna down, steps in, everything stowed, cords and hoses disconnected, all compartments latched.
Confirm the L.P. (propane) valve is OFF for driving and the cooktop is off.
tips: Keep the InfoCase (manuals, serial numbers) in the van — you're most likely to need it on the road.

sections.title: After Your Trip
sections.quick: How to shut the van down when you're back so it stays fresh and ready for next time.
steps: Dump and rinse the tanks — black first, then gray — and add tank treatment.
Turn the fridge OFF and prop the door open so it doesn't get moldy.
Turn OFF the water pump, the inverter, the water heater, and the L.P. (propane) valve.
Take out all food and trash; wipe down the counters and sink.
Unplug shore power and stow the cord; drain and stow the water hoses.
Sweep out and crack a roof vent so it airs out in storage.
tips: Leaving the fridge door propped and a vent cracked is the trick to no mold or musty smell.
warnings: If it will be below freezing in storage, drain the water system or winterize it so the pipes don't burst.

sections.title: OnePlace Monitor
sections.quick: The van's status panel, above the sliding door. Press a button to read tank levels or battery, run the water pump, start/stop the generator, or turn the water heater on.
steps: LEVELS → press "Tank" to see fresh / gray / black on the screen (shown as 1/3, 2/3, Full — there's usually a little more than it reads). Press "Battery" for the house + chassis charge.
WATER → "Pump" turns the water pump on/off. "Heater Electric" runs the water heater on 120V (shore/generator); "Heater L.P." runs it on propane.
GENSET → press and hold "Start" to start the generator, "Stop" to stop it. "Meter" shows generator info (hours, etc.).
tips: Tank gauges read low on purpose — "Full" means full, but 2/3 usually has more left than it shows.
The water pump switch is also on the Water Center panel.

photos.caption: OnePlace monitor — GenSet, Water, and Levels buttons

sections.title: Power Control System
sections.quick: An automatic power-load manager. It spreads the van's electrical load so running several things at once doesn't trip the breaker. You normally don't touch it.
steps: Leave it on / automatic — it works by itself on shore power (15 / 20 / 30-amp) or the generator.
To view readings, press SCROLL to cycle through them and SELECT to choose one.
If an appliance briefly pauses while several are running, that's the PCS balancing the load — normal, nothing to fix.
tips: If a breaker still trips, run fewer big things at once — A/C plus microwave together is the usual culprit on a small hookup.

photos.caption: Power Control System — green LCD with SELECT / SCROLL

sections.title: Water Center (Nautilus)
sections.quick: The plumbing control panel, behind the passenger rear door. Labeled levers set how water flows; it also has the water pump switch, the Eco-hot valve, and the exterior light switch.
steps: Set the LEVERS to match the picture labels on the panel: "Dry Camping" for normal onboard-tank use, "City Water" when hooked to a spigot, "Power Fill Tank" to fill the fresh tank, "Winterize" for storage.
To FILL the fresh tank: attach a drinking-water hose to the Tank Fill Inlet, set the levers to "Power Fill Tank," fill it (don't walk away), then set back to "Dry Camping."
The Water Pump switch (top right) turns the pump on/off.
Eco-hot (fast hot water without waste): with the pump on, turn the Eco-hot valve to "Pre-heat"; after about 6 seconds the water is hot, then turn it back to "Normal."
The exterior utility light switch (lights the dump/wash area) is on this panel too.
tips: Turn the water pump OFF when you leave the van or drive.
warnings: On city water, use an inline pressure regulator (50 psi max) to protect the plumbing.

photos.caption: Water Center (Nautilus) panel behind the passenger rear door

sections.title: L.P. Valve & Tank Heaters
sections.quick: Two rocker switches near the monitor panel (they light up when on): the L.P. (propane) valve and the holding-tank heaters.
steps: L.P. VALVE — turns the propane supply on/off for the cooktop and Truma heater. Turn it ON to cook or run propane heat; OFF when done and always while driving/refueling.
HOLDING TANK HEATER — turns the heating pads on the fresh/gray/black tanks on/off. Use it ONLY in freezing weather to keep tanks and lines from freezing; turn it back off otherwise.
warnings: The tank heaters draw a LOT of power — only run them while plugged into shore power or with the engine running. On battery alone they'll drain the house battery (won't last overnight).

photos.caption: L.P. Valve and Holding Tank Heater rocker switches

sections.title: Battery System
sections.quick: The house runs on an AGM battery (it powers the 12V things: lights, water pump, fridge). An INVERTER makes 120V power, but ONLY for the TV, the sound bar, and the 2 outlets inside the cabinet above the TV. EVERYTHING else — all other outlets, the A/C, and the microwave — needs the GENERATOR running, the VEHICLE ENGINE running (while driving), or SHORE POWER.
steps: The inverter switch is above the sliding door. To turn it On or Off, press and hold the switch for 1–2 seconds, then release — a green light shows when it's On.
Inverter On powers the TV, the sound bar, and the 2 outlets in the cabinet above the TV.
For any other outlet, the A/C, or the microwave: run the generator, run the engine (driving), or use shore power.
Check the OnePlace panel (above the sliding door) for battery charge and tank levels.
Turn the inverter Off when done to save the battery.
tips: Easy rule: TV + sound bar + the 2 cabinet-above-TV outlets = inverter/battery. Everything else = generator, engine, or shore power.
The AGM battery recharges four ways: driving (engine alternator), running the generator, shore power, and the rooftop SOLAR PANELS on sunny days.
The fridge (12V), lights, water pump, and fans all sip from the battery even when parked — so recharge daily when boondocking.
If an inverter outlet has no power, the inverter's GFCI may have tripped — press the RESET button on the GFCI outlet, then try again.
Solar is automatic: the solar charge controller tops up the battery on sunny days (a green light means it's charging) — nothing to switch on.
warnings: AGM batteries should not be run flat — recharge before the battery gets low (drive, generator, solar, or shore power).

photos.caption: The inverter switch (above the sliding door)
OnePlace monitor — battery charge and tank levels
The 2 inverter-powered outlets inside the cabinet above the TV
Solar charge controller

sections.title: Generator
sections.quick: The under-floor Onan generator makes the 120V power that runs the A/C, the microwave, and ALL the regular outlets (the inverter only covers the TV, sound bar, and cabinet-above-TV outlet). Start/stop it with the GENSET buttons on the OnePlace panel. If it trips or won't start, use the RESET steps below.
steps: Before starting: turn OFF big 120V loads (A/C, microwave) so the generator starts under light load.
On the OnePlace panel, press and HOLD the GENSET "Start" button until the generator fires, then release. It may crank a few seconds. (The "Meter" button shows generator info.)
Let it warm up ~1–2 minutes before switching on the A/C or microwave.
To stop: turn off A/C/microwave first, then press the GENSET "Stop" button.
tips: The generator itself is in a compartment underneath the van. Never run it while sleeping without working CO detectors, and never in an enclosed space.
Keep at least 1/4 tank of fuel — the generator shares the van's gas tank and will stop to protect the engine's fuel supply.
warnings: Generator exhaust contains carbon monoxide, which is deadly. Keep windows/doors on the exhaust side closed and make sure the CO detector is working.

subsections.title: RESET the Onan generator (won't start, or tripped off)
steps: Turn OFF all 120V loads inside (A/C, microwave, chargers).
Go to the generator unit underneath the van (exterior access panel/compartment).
On the generator's own face/control area, find the CIRCUIT BREAKER / RESET button (a push-button, often red or black, sometimes labeled "RESET" or a rocker breaker).
If it's popped out/tripped, press it firmly IN to reset it.
At the generator (or interior switch), press and HOLD the STOP side of the switch for about 3–5 seconds to clear any fault, then release.
Now press and HOLD the START/PRIME side until the generator starts, then release once it's running smoothly.
If it still won't start, check: fuel level (need ~1/4 tank), and look for a blinking fault light on the generator — count the blinks and match to the Onan operator card in the InfoCase.
warnings: If the generator repeatedly trips or won't start after one reset attempt, STOP and call the owner. Do not force it.

photos.caption: Generator compartment underneath the van
The reset/breaker button on the generator unit
Interior START/STOP switch

sections.title: Plugging In
sections.quick: "Shore power" means plugging the van into a campground outlet (30-amp). This runs EVERYTHING (including A/C and microwave) AND recharges the battery. Turn the campground breaker OFF before plugging/unplugging. Power extension cords and outlet adapters are stored in the rear compartment (open the passenger back door — above and beside the water inlets, same place as the water hoses).
steps: Open the PASSENGER BACK DOOR. In the rear compartment above/beside the water tank inlets you'll find the shore cord, power EXTENSION CORDS, OUTLET ADAPTERS, and the water hoses.
At the campground pedestal, switch the breaker OFF.
Plug the cord into the van's sidewall inlet (driver side) and the other end into the 30-amp pedestal outlet. Use an adapter from the compartment if the outlet doesn't match.
Switch the pedestal breaker back ON.
You now have full power, and the battery is charging automatically.
tips: Everything you need for hookups — power cords, adapters, and water hoses — lives together in that rear compartment behind the passenger back door.
Use a 30-amp outlet when possible. On a household (15/20-amp) outlet with an adapter, you can't run the A/C and microwave at the same time.
warnings: Use the proper heavy-duty cords/adapters from the compartment — don't use a light household extension cord for the main hookup, as undersized cords can overheat and cause a fire.
Make sure all prongs are fully seated and dry before switching power on.

photos.caption: The 30-amp power inlet on the driver-side wall
Rear compartment (passenger back door): cords, adapters, and water hoses by the water inlets

sections.title: Water & Pump
sections.quick: Water comes from the onboard fresh tank (turn the WATER PUMP switch ON to get pressure at the faucets) or from a city hose hookup. The pump switch is on the monitor panel and the Water Center panel — an indicator light shows when it's on.
steps: To use tank water: turn the WATER PUMP switch ON (on the monitor panel or Water Center panel). Water flows when you open a faucet.
Open a faucet briefly — you'll hear the pump run to build pressure, then it quiets when pressurized.
If using a campground water spigot instead, connect a drinking-water hose to the city water inlet — then you don't need the pump.
Check the fresh water level on the OnePlace monitor panel.
tips: Turn the pump OFF when you leave the van, so a drip or open valve can't drain the tank or flood.
Conserve water when boondocking: shorter showers, turn off while soaping.

photos.caption: Water pump switch (green light = on)
The Water Center panel

sections.title: Outdoor Shower
sections.quick: There's an exterior shower/wash station (hot & cold) behind the rear passenger-side door — perfect for rinsing sandy feet, gear, or the dog after the beach. Water pump ON (or city water), pull out the sprayer, set the temperature, spray. Turn it off and stow when done.
steps: Open the exterior compartment behind the rear passenger-side door to reach the wash station.
Make sure the water pump is ON (or you're connected to city water).
Connect/pull out the sprayer hose, set the hot/cold mixing valves to a comfortable temperature.
Use the trigger/valve on the sprayer to control the water.
When done, shut off the valves, drain and stow the hose, and close the compartment.
tips: Great for a quick rinse without tracking sand or mud inside the van.
Turn the water pump OFF afterward so nothing keeps the system pressurized.
warnings: In freezing weather, don't leave the sprayer hose connected/full — it can freeze.

photos.caption: Exterior shower/wash station behind the rear passenger door

sections.title: Water Heater
sections.quick: Gives you hot water. Make sure the water heater is FILLED with water before turning it on (never run it dry). Turn it on with the OnePlace "Heater" button — Electric (uses shore/generator power) or L.P. (uses propane) — wait ~20–30 min. Turn it off when you don't need hot water.
steps: Make sure the water system is pressurized (pump on or city water connected) and the water heater is filled — open a hot faucet until water flows steadily, then close it.
Confirm the Water Center panel is NOT in "bypass/winterize" (the red lever should be in the normal, not bypass, position).
On the OnePlace panel, press "Heater Electric" (runs on 120V shore/generator) OR "Heater L.P." (runs on propane) to turn the water heater on.
Wait about 20–30 minutes for the water to heat.
Turn it OFF when you're done to conserve power/propane.
warnings: NEVER turn on the water heater when it's empty (in bypass or winterized mode) — it can be damaged.

photos.caption: Water heater on/off control
Red bypass lever — normal (not bypass) position for use

sections.title: Toilet
sections.quick: Flush with the foot/hand pedal — a little water first, then flush. OUR TRICK: use a bowl liner for solids so we fill the black tank much slower and dump less often. Only RV-safe toilet paper. Nothing else goes in.
steps: For a normal flush: hold the flush lever/pedal — add a little water to the bowl first, do your business, then flush fully so waste clears into the black tank.

OWNER'S BOWL-LINER TRICK (to save black tank space when boondocking): before use, drop an RV toilet bowl liner bag into the bowl. After solids-only use, seal the bag and dispose in the trash instead of flushing — this keeps the black tank from filling quickly.
Add a little water and a scoop/pod of holding-tank treatment after dumping to control odor.
tips: Keep a small stock of RV-safe toilet paper and bowl liners in the bathroom cabinet.
Liquids-only flushing between liner uses keeps things fresh without filling the tank.
warnings: Do NOT flush regular facial/household tissue, wipes (even "flushable"), sanitary products, or any non-dissolving items — they clog the tank.
Do NOT pour bleach or harsh chemicals in the toilet — they damage the seals.

photos.caption: The toilet and flush pedal
Bowl liner in place

sections.title: Gray/Black Tanks
sections.quick: When tanks are full (check the monitor panel), dump at a dump station. Connect the sewer hose, empty BLACK first, then GRAY (gray rinses the hose). Never leave valves open while parked. Wear the gloves in the wet-bay.
steps: Check tank levels on the OnePlace monitor panel before dumping.
Put on the disposable gloves. Remove the sewer hose from its storage compartment.
Remove the cap from the sewage drain outlet and firmly attach the sewer hose. Put the other end securely into the dump station opening.
Open the BLACK tank valve first with a quick pull. Let it fully drain, then close it.
Open the GRAY tank valve next (this rinses black residue from the hose). Close it when empty.
Rinse/flush if a rinse connection is available, store the hose, add tank treatment through the toilet.
tips: Dump before travel days when possible — you don't want to haul the extra weight.
Rule of thumb: BLACK before GRAY, never both at once.
warnings: Never open the gray valve before the black is drained and closed, or you'll back sewage into the gray side.

photos.caption: Sewer hose storage and dump valves
Black (left) and gray valve pulls

sections.title: Refrigerator
sections.quick: The fridge runs automatically on 12V from the house battery (no generator needed). Turn it on and set the dial around 3–4. Give it a few hours to cool before loading lots of food. Leave it on the whole trip.
steps: Turn the fridge power ON and set the thermostat to about 3–4 (midrange).
Let it cool for 3–4 hours (or overnight) before fully loading with food.
To turn off (end of trip), set the thermostat to zero/off and prop the door open to prevent mold.
tips: Pre-chill food before loading; the fridge cools better when it's not cooling warm items.
Keep the door closed on hot days; open it as little as possible.

sections.title: Microwave
sections.quick: The microwave needs the GENERATOR running, SHORE POWER, or the VEHICLE ENGINE running (while driving). It does NOT run on the inverter/battery. Don't run it and the A/C at the same time on a small (15/20-amp) hookup.
steps: Get 120V power first: start the generator, plug into shore power, or run the engine. The microwave does NOT work on the inverter.
Use it like a normal microwave.
If it won't power on, you don't have generator/shore/engine power — start the generator or plug in.
warnings: The microwave will NOT run on the inverter/battery — it needs the generator, shore power, or the engine running.
On a small household (15/20-amp) hookup, don't run the microwave and A/C together — it will trip the breaker.

sections.title: Cooktop
sections.quick: The cooktop is PROPANE. Turn the PROPANE on first — the LP switch is above the side door — then light a burner. No generator or shore power needed. Turn the burners AND the propane OFF when done, and always ventilate.
steps: Turn ON the LP (propane) switch above the side door.
Turn on a vent fan or crack a window for fresh air.
Push in and turn the burner knob to light it (hold until the flame catches), then adjust the flame.
When finished, turn the burners OFF, then turn the LP (propane) switch OFF.
tips: No generator or shore power needed — the cooktop runs on propane.
warnings: Never use the cooktop to heat the van and never leave it unattended — risk of carbon monoxide/asphyxiation. Always ventilate while cooking.
Turn the propane OFF while driving and when refueling (see Propane & Stove for safety).

photos.caption: The propane cooktop
LP (propane) switch above the side door

sections.title: Roof A/C
sections.quick: The roof A/C is the main way to cool a parked van. It needs REAL 120V power: the GENERATOR running, SHORE POWER, or the VEHICLE ENGINE running (while driving). It will NOT run on the inverter/battery. Select Low Cool / High Cool / Auto and set the temperature. There's also a Bluetooth app to control it.
steps: Get 120V power first: start the generator (see Generator card), plug into shore power, or run the engine. The A/C does NOT work on the inverter.
Press the selector to "Low Cool", "High Cool", or "Auto".
Set the temperature to comfortable. The A/C cycles on/off to hold the temperature.
Aim the louvers where you want airflow.
tips: On cooler nights (below ~75°F), set the temp to the midpoint, not the coldest — running it too cold can ice up the coil and stop cooling.
If it ices up and airflow drops, run the fan on High until the ice clears.
The A/C can be controlled from a phone via its Bluetooth app (see Resources).
warnings: The A/C will NOT run on the inverter/battery — if it won't start, you need the generator, shore power, or the engine running.
If the GENERATOR is running but the A/C still won't start, the BREAKER on the generator unit (underneath the van) may be tripped — reset it (see the Generator card's RESET steps).

photos.caption: Roof A/C control / thermostat

sections.title: Dash A/C
sections.quick: The dash A/C is the van's automotive air conditioning — it only works while the ENGINE is running, so it's for staying cool on the road. It's small and weak for the living space, so don't rely on it to cool a parked van (use the roof A/C for that).
steps: Start the engine.
Use the dash climate controls (fan, temperature, A/C button) just like a car.
To cool a parked van, use the Roof A/C instead.
tips: When it's really hot, run BOTH the dash A/C (engine) and the roof A/C to cool the van down faster.

sections.title: Heater (Hybrid)
sections.quick: The heater is a hybrid (Truma) — it makes warm forced air AND hot water, and can run on PROPANE, on 120V ELECTRIC (shore/generator), or both ("Mix"). Use the round Truma dial near the panels: turn the knob to set the temperature; on-screen a flame icon = gas heat, a plug icon = electric.
steps: For propane heat, make sure the LP (propane) switch above the side door is ON.
On the Truma dial (the round control with the flame/plug screen), press the knob for ~3 seconds to turn it on.

Turn the knob to set the room temperature. Use the menu to choose the energy mode: Gas (flame icon = propane), Electric (plug icon = needs shore/generator), or Mix. The back-arrow button steps back a menu.
The same system heats your hot water — set the hot water temperature in the menu too.
tips: Gas (propane) mode works anywhere with no hookup. Electric mode needs shore power or the generator.
On very cold nights, "Mix" uses both for faster, steadier heat.
warnings: When running propane heat overnight, keep a vent cracked and make sure the CO detector is working.

photos.caption: Truma heater control panel (near the monitor panel)

sections.title: Sound Bar
sections.quick: The sound bar is one of the few things powered by the INVERTER — turn the inverter ON (switch above the sliding door), then pair your phone over Bluetooth. Use the "Source" button to switch inputs: WHITE light = TV, BLUE light = Bluetooth. No generator needed for this.
steps: Turn the INVERTER on (switch above the sliding door), then turn the sound bar ON with the remote.
Press the "Source" button (the RIGHT button, the one with the left-arrow) to change the input. WHITE light = TV, BLUE light = Bluetooth.
With the input set to Bluetooth (blue), press and HOLD the "Source" button for a few seconds until the BLUE light is PULSATING — that means it's in pairing mode.
On your phone, open Bluetooth settings and select the sound bar. Once connected, play audio and it comes out the sound bar.
tips: If it won't pair, turn the sound bar off and on, "forget" any old device on your phone, then repeat the press-and-hold to get the blue light pulsating again.

photos.caption: Sound bar remote — the "Source" button (right, left-arrow) switches TV (white) vs Bluetooth (blue)

sections.title: TV
sections.quick: The TV runs off the INVERTER (one of the few things that does) — turn the inverter ON (switch above the sliding door), then turn on the TV with its remote. No generator needed. Use SOURCE/INPUT to switch between antenna, HDMI, or a streaming device.
steps: Turn the INVERTER on (switch above the sliding door). No generator needed for the TV.
Turn on the TV with the remote.
Press SOURCE/INPUT to pick: over-the-air Antenna, or HDMI (streaming stick / game console).
For local channels, make sure the TV antenna is raised/on, then run a channel scan in the TV menu.
tips: Cell/Wi-Fi streaming works best; over-the-air antenna reception depends on where you're parked.

photos.caption: TV and remote

sections.title: Awning
sections.quick: The Carefree awning control has two rockers: LEFT = ON/OFF, RIGHT = RETRACT/EXTEND. Two rules: (1) the LEFT switch must be ON before extend/retract will work, and (2) the PARKING BRAKE must be ON. Safety features: if you release the parking brake while the awning is out, OR if the wind gets too strong, the awning retracts itself automatically. There's an awning LIGHT switch right next to it.
steps: Set the PARKING BRAKE — the awning will not operate without it.
On the awning control, flip the LEFT switch to ON.
Hold the RIGHT switch toward EXTEND to open the awning (or RETRACT to close it); release when it's where you want it.
To turn the awning light on/off, use the LIGHT switch next to the awning control.
When you're done adjusting, you can flip the LEFT switch back to OFF.
tips: Extend it partway to shade the side windows — it helps keep the van cooler on hot days.
warnings: ALWAYS retract the awning before driving, and in strong wind, rain, or storms.
Auto-retract: releasing the parking brake with the awning out will make it close on its own, and so will high wind — keep hands and objects clear of the awning arms.

photos.caption: Carefree Awning Control — LEFT = ON/OFF, RIGHT = RETRACT/EXTEND (light switch beside it)

sections.title: Supplies & Gear
sections.quick: Extra gear and supplies are stocked in the van for you to use — here's what's onboard and roughly where to find it. Please tidy up and put things back when you're done.
tips: This list is a starting point — edit it in the content file to match exactly what's stocked.

photos.caption: Where the extra gear is stored

items: Extra bedding, pillows, blankets — overhead bunk
Cups, plates, bowls, utensils — galley drawers
Basic cooking utensils, dish soap, sponge — galley
Leveling blocks + wheel chocks — rear compartment

sections.title: Leveling
sections.quick: Park on the flattest spot you can find. Use the leveling blocks under the tires for small slopes (drive up onto them). Set the parking brake, chock a wheel if on any incline. A level van means the fridge works well and you sleep better.
steps: Pick the flattest available spot. Check level using the bubble level (or a phone level app) inside.
For minor slopes, place the leveling blocks in front of/behind the low-side tires and slowly drive onto them.
Set the parking brake firmly.
On any slope, place a wheel chock for extra safety.
tips: Level side-to-side matters most for sleeping comfort and the fridge.

sections.title: Driving Basics
sections.quick: It drives like a tall van. Watch your HEIGHT (about 9'4" — mind low clearances, drive-throughs, garages, tree branches). Use the mirrors and back-up camera. Take corners wide. Before moving: everything stowed, steps/antenna in, cords disconnected.
steps: PRE-DRIVE CHECK: inside items stowed, roof vents/antenna down, TV antenna down, steps in, windows closed, fridge latched.
OUTSIDE: shore cord unplugged and stored, water hose disconnected, all compartment doors latched, nothing left behind.
Adjust mirrors and seat. Note the height (~9'4") for clearances.
Drive smoothly — it's taller and heavier than a car, so brake earlier and take turns wide.
warnings: Low clearances are the #1 rental RV damage. When unsure of a clearance, DON'T risk it — find another route.

photos.caption: Pre-drive walk-around checklist

sections.title: Propane & Stove
sections.quick: Propane powers the stove (and furnace). Turn the propane valve on to cook, off when done and when driving/refueling. If you ever smell gas: no flames, no switches — open windows, turn off propane, and get everyone out.
steps: To cook: make sure the propane supply valve is ON, light the burner per the stove instructions.
Turn burners OFF and the propane supply OFF when finished.
Turn propane OFF while driving and always at gas stations.
warnings: If you smell propane (rotten-egg smell): do NOT touch light switches or create sparks. Open windows/doors, shut off the propane, exit the van, and don't return until it's clear.
Keep the CO/propane detector powered — never disable it.

sections.title: Dos & Don'ts
sections.quick: A quick cheat sheet of the things that keep the van happy.
tips: DO turn the inverter and water pump OFF when you leave the van.
DO keep an eye on the battery charge and tank levels on the OnePlace panel (above the sliding door).
DO use only RV-safe toilet paper and the bowl liners.
DO dump black before gray, and dump before big travel days.
DON'T flush wipes, tissue, or anything besides RV paper.
DON'T run the water heater dry (empty).
DON'T run A/C + microwave together on a small hookup.
DON'T forget the height (~9'4") — watch clearances.
DON'T leave the propane on while driving or refueling.

sections.title: Emergency
sections.quick: Save the owner's number in your phone before you leave. For roadside issues use the roadside assistance number. In a real emergency call 911. Breaker/reset locations are listed below.
steps: OWNER (call/text anytime): __________  (fill in)
Roadside assistance / insurance: __________  (fill in policy #)
If a 120V outlet is dead: check the inverter GFCI reset, then the house 120V breakers.
If the generator won't run: see the Generator RESET card.
If nothing electrical works: check the main house breaker and the battery charge (may just need charging — drive, run the generator, or plug in).
tips: House 120V circuit breakers and fuses are in the load center (interior panel) — labeled inside the door.
Take a photo and text the owner rather than guessing on anything you're unsure about.

sections.title: FAQ
sections.quick: Quick answers to the questions guests ask most (thanks to the Travato Owners community for many of these).

faq.q: Why does the OnePlace tank/battery gauge seem to drop fast?
faq.a: It reads the next level DOWN as soon as you use a little. Water/gray/black show Full, 2/3, 1/3, Empty; LP shows 3/4, 1/2, 1/4, E. So "3/4" really means "somewhere between 3/4 and full." It's normal.

faq.q: What runs on the battery (DC) vs. needs generator/shore (AC)?
faq.a: On battery (DC): fridge, lights, TV, sound bar, ceiling/Max fans, water pump, and the OnePlace display. Needs 120V AC (generator, shore, or engine): the roof A/C and the microwave.

faq.q: Water sprays from the shower connection on the wall when I flush the toilet — why?
faq.a: The wall shower valve is left ON. Turn that shower valve OFF; otherwise running the water pump over-pressurizes that line and water comes out the connection.

faq.q: I can't insert or remove the shower hose in the wall port.
faq.a: There's pressure in the line. Turn the water pump OFF (and city water off), then open an inside faucet to relieve pressure. Then push in the chrome sleeve to release, or push the quick-connect in to insert.

faq.q: It's really hot — how do I get it cool?
faq.a: Manage the sun: put in the windshield/cab-door covers, close the shades on the sunny side, and extend the awning to shade the windows. Run the cab A/C (engine) plus the roof A/C to catch up, and use 12V/USB fans. The A/C only cools about 20°F below the outside temperature.

faq.q: Should I use the onboard tank or "city water" hookup?
faq.a: Most owners use the onboard fresh tank and just switch the water pump ON only while using water. Bonus: if you ever hear the pump running when no tap is open, that's your early warning of a leak.

faq.q: Where's the light switch for the driver's-side dump/service area?
faq.a: It's an unlabeled switch on the back wall near the water pump switch (owners call it the "GSDL"). The switch on the other side of the pump switch runs the back-wall light.

faq.q: What's in the InfoCase and should I keep it in the van?
faq.a: Yes—keep it in the van. It has the owner's manuals, the ProMaster manual, the window sticker, and a component sheet with model/serial numbers you'll want if you ever call for help on the road.
